meta.setting: High pine ridge above French Gulch roads
meta.rdr_clone_target: PS3-era RDR-clone western art, grounded in smoke, distance, and signal pressure

research_grounding.title: NPS The Wintu
research_grounding.use: Clear Creek land context and fire stewardship, handled respectfully.

research_grounding.title: Karuk Tribe
research_grounding.use: Northern California stewardship and cultural burning context.

image_prompt.subject: a ridge lookout beside a small rock windbreak, using a mirror and cloth signal while smoke columns rise in three distant valleys
image_prompt.scene: high pine ridge with sharp wind; bedroll, brass spyglass, saddled horse, hawks over a road far below, and dust plumes that may be riders or cattle
image_prompt.composition: square high-vista plate with lookout in foreground and multiple readable distance clues; smoke columns and dust plumes create competing messages
image_prompt.camera: wide third-person game camera from just behind the lookout shoulder, high enough to show the road web below
image_prompt.hand_cut_note: Make the distance legible; the page is about deciding what far-away signs mean.

style_modifiers.medium: PS3-era open-world western game art, early-HD in-engine vista and signal scene
render_traits: slightly simplified lookout, horse, and ridge silhouettes
modest texture resolution on rock, cloth, brass, pine, dust, and smoke
hard ambient occlusion under windbreak stones, bedroll, saddle, and boots
clear high-country light with harsh wind feel
believable 1899 signal tools and trail gear
distance-reading composition rather than heroic overlook
style_modifiers.palette: pine green, ridge stone gray, smoke blue, dust tan, cloth red-faded, brass dull gold, saddle brown, and hard sky blue.

text_rendering.instructions: No readable signs, labels, map marks, or text.

negative_prompt: copy of commercial game characters, missions, towns, or logos
cropped smoke columns or cropped lookout

self_check: Reads as PS3-era western game art at square page-card scale.
The lookout, signal cloth, mirror, horse, smoke columns, and distant road are readable.
Every visible prop belongs plausibly to 1899 high-country signaling.
The image makes distance and interpretation the main pressure.
No copied game characters, readable modern text, or inherited non-western symbols.
